speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Jacobs, we were talking about your time in the ghetto. Describe what a typical day was for you from when you woke up in the morning to when you went to sleep at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I know, that we went to work, and we did look forward to come home to avoid any kind of trouble. How much you could avoid, you always run in some trouble, but you tried to escape, or whatever it is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the-- and you did look for the next day to survive. And you have something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the ghetto? In a shop. We worked for the Germans, for the public and for also military, very little, just what the Germans organized a shop for the people, the German, the way for the SS to make uniforms, all kind of stuff like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were living with your parents at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. In that time, we had to move into the ghetto. We used to live on [NON-ENGLISH] 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many hours would you work there at the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was about eight hours, if I'm not mistaking, or more. I don't know. I didn't recall correctly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat typically in a day in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mostly potatoes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were some people, they had a little bit more. We got from the Germans what we worked for them. They give us some extra bread. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was their own, not the-- that it belonged to us. But some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any activities, anything organized in the ghetto by the Jewish community-- cultural events or social events of any sort that come to mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I know. Maybe indirectly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But nobody should know about this. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any actions in the ghetto before the liquidation? Before the ghetto was liquidated, were there any actions or pogroms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me. One time, they made a-- a Purim [NON-ENGLISH] I call this [NON-ENGLISH], if you know what I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which translates as what? Would the word 'action' be a translation for [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. A Purim, and they took out-- they-- they come, the SS, the Gestapo. And they said we had to bring out all the gold, silver, and money, and whatever we have. And in-- in order to do this, they-- right away, they killed three people, that everybody would be afraid and running into the houses and bringing out whatever they have. They killed in that time, his name was, [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH], a young boy. He was about 15 years old. And then another man, he was-- he was in the ghetto, in Tomaszow. Actually, he was from Lodz. They were killed. And everybody did run into the house and bring it out, like silver, whatever they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just want to try to pin down or describe the scene a little bit more. The Germans came into the ghetto, and how did they make people aware that they were to assemble? Did they yell instructions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, through the Jewish police and they'd run it. This was not unusual that you could hear right away, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe what then took place.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on-- on the street [NON-ENGLISH]. We had to assemble, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and there, everybody-- whoever didn't brought it out, they said if they're going to go in into the house and find something, right away, they'll be killed. I remember there is one [NON-ENGLISH]. They had a jewelry, a very big-- they're very rich people-- a jewelry. And he used to come always into our house. And he would-- some people, they had afraid even to give it. As soon you're going to give it, they could kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what he did, he throw it on the street [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like garbage. And after they left, I remember we went out. He called me because we were friend. And we went down the street, and we find something. He still lives in Israel, in Haifa. I just talked to him last year about this, that incident. You had spoken, or you had said at the beginning of this [NON-ENGLISH] or at the-- after the Jews had been assembled, three Jews were shot. Did you witness their shooting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right near me. Samuelson was killed right near me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe his killing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He shot-- his name was Kemp, a Gestapo. He just took him-- oh, dear. He just, with the pistol, he-- here, mostly, they shot here, right here. And he did roll over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was-- was there any particular reason to pick Samuelson?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I could be picked just the same as anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you were given instructions to go and get your valuables.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you or your family members bring?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I remember we brought the candlelight, the silver, and some other things, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] whatever we could. Sometime, you forgot to-- to bring something out too.

speaker: interviewer
text: In their instructions, did the Germans say that you had to come back in a certain amount of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, as fast as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they said, go, what then took place? When they said, go bring your valuables--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what did it look like? Describe what it looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody did panic. It's unbelievable panic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what that panic looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody did run, and it didn't take minutes. Everybody came back. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But they didn't go later on into the houses. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You had said earlier that in the Judenraete, in the Jewish council, there were some that were good, and there were some that were bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you speak to that in a little bit more detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's even the Jewish police, some were very good and some were bad--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you elaborate?

speaker: interviewee
text: --unfortunately. Kapos, Jewish kapos. Unfortunately, that was true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you provide some more details about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was one guy in our place. Even he was a neighbor of us. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a terrible man. He said, should one hair from my head fall out, I rather see 10 or 100 Jews killed. He was not bad to me, personally. It's a matter of fact, he wanted to make me as a Jewish policeman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I'm not the type. It's not me. And he was very much in contact with that [NON-ENGLISH], like friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was [NON-ENGLISH] a Jewish police, or was he in the Judenraete?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish policeman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was a terrible man.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say he was terrible, you've provided that anecdote about what he had said about one hair. Did you ever witness him, or were you familiar with his behavior towards the other Jews in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were in the first camp in Blizyn, we did work in the shop. They give us thread in there. And everybody had to look after this like, I don't know, not to get lost. And, you know, they didn't give the threads. Make it. We weren't going to make it. Everybody, they try to keep it so that it doesn't get lost. I remember a boy from 16 years, he was from Bialystok. And he forgot to put it out that-- that thread. And he had a small spool of thread. And he had it in his pocket. And after the shift, we went out, and that same [NON-ENGLISH], he noticed that spool of this. He went over to the shop fuehrer, the German. His name was Meyerlink, a German. And he said, and they give him a gift. That boy, over next day, for this, he got killed, that boy, a 16-year-old boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, did [NON-ENGLISH] have any particular status? Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the same thing, but he was like a, let's say, he collaborated with the Germans, in one word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any point during your time in the ghetto when you had information of what was taking place at other locations to Jews in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had to-- had to do it for themself. That did take place some things, you know, how to escape, or whether there is some people. But some people did escape, you know, from the ghetto, and lived outside the ghetto. Later on, when that happened, we got to know. But everybody was so organized secretly, because you never knew what the other person is doing

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the liquidation of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The liquidation was November-- you mean when they liquidate everything?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I--

speaker: interviewee
text: November the 30th. And it come out on a Saturday. They liquidated half of the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm just going to stop you there for a moment. Of what year, November 30th?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, that was. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- that's right, November the 30th, 1942. And they send it to we didn't know where. And then Sunday-- it was Sunday. That was on a Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to stop you there for a moment. I'm sorry, but can you give some more detail as to what the liquidation looked like? Were the Jews in the ghetto given any advance notice? Or did the Germans just come into the ghetto and start yelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- what took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were not any notice, as far as I know. And they just came in, and they took part of Tomaszow, where the ghetto is. Like the ghetto were not so big, like in Lodz or in Warsaw. But part of the streets where the ghetto is, that-- that part goes out on Saturday. And then-- which I didn't see it, because you couldn't go. It was like a spare. You couldn't go on the street. And they took one part. And then the first-- in the 2nd of November was the second part where they took it out. This, I remember correctly, the second part, because we always make a memorial day the 2nd of November. And that was liquidate. And there was left only the people-- the workers like me and others in a separate place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the ghetto was liquidated, the Germans, from your description, the Germans came in. Did they come in alone, or did they come in-- did they have other people assisting them in this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure, the Ukraines, the Latvians, the Latvians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jewish police involved in that liquidation?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were involved to a certain they-- let's say they-- some Jewish policemen, they went to the train station with the people, what they thought they going to be spared. On the station, they had to go in in the-- in the cars too, the police, the Jewish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that liquidation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I was told. And, it's a matter of fact, we didn't see, later on, those policemen. So that means they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what happened to your family members in that liquidation.

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- we had cards. And the cards were stamped from the Gestapo, like we are working for-- whoever did work for the Gestapo, for the Germans. And I had a card like this too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went with my parents and with my sister, my youngest sister, to our little church, where they were assembled. And there they'd stay-- the Gestapo, only the Gestapo that stayed there, with page-- with the pages, yeah? What you-- how do you call this? The-- you know, when they want to-- like, they're the-- With weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weapons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clubs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clubs. That's right. Let it stay there, and they did look over the cards. So whoever had a stamp, they sent it into the church. Whoever didn't-- my father, my mother didn't have. I had. My sister had. And they sended me over. In that time, they-- my parents, they sended another-- another way, which they, later on, they went to the train and go to Treblinka. That was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were on your way to the church--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you walking there with your parents? Who were  you--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my parents, from the house where we used to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else were you with? Your parents and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm talking about of your family members.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family members, they were before. They were before in the church. Not in the church, on the lot from the church. And some of them, they went already. It's a matter of fact, when we came into the church lot, I saw my brother-in-law. And then he said that he wanted to go with his wife. So they said, OK. They took him out. And they sent him to-- with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had authorization to stay, to remain in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because he had the stamp. He was already in the churchyard. So that means that he was authorized.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you walking alone with your parents to that assembly to the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I know the whole town, but I'm saying, were your-- was your brother Nojech with you? Or was Haskell with you when you were walking there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Haskell was in Russia. Yeah. And I did walk, my brother, Nojech, and his wife, and his daughter, the six-year-old daughter. And they were taken away. And we had to go into the yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any conversation or discussion that you can recall with your mother or your father as you were standing there waiting to go through this process?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The only thing that I know, my sister, while she is in Toronto, she didn't want to leave Mother. And when you come near the Gestapo, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they just clubbed you. You had to go here, and you had to go here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you in a line-up waiting to pass by a Gestapo person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they seated at desks--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or tables or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, just standing. Yeah, you just didn't know what to do. Whatever it should be, it should be over in minutes. We were so afraid just looking at them with the dogs that stay in-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] oh, dear, it wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you last see your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we walked there to the church. That was the last. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remained in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, till we went to Blizyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents went into the church?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We went into the churchyard, and they went to go farther. Right away, they went right to the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was your understanding that those trains went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know anything. We didn't understand anything. Later on, we found out. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to the ghetto, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, we had to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your regular work in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: --shop. Approximately what percentage of the ghetto's population was deported during that liquidation that you've just described?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them. Maybe about, I would-- I don't think so even 500 people were left.

speaker: interviewer
text: 500 out of a total of about how many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within that time-- in that time, you know, before the war, was about 15,000 Jews. And later on, during the war, a lot of people did come from all of over-- from Lodz, from Lezne, from all over, they did come to Tomaszow. I would say it was about, for sure about double, 30,000 or more. I don't know exactly, because everybody, they thought, here is better. In Tomaszow, will be better, which it was very bad  in Lodz. We had a lot of people from Lodz, Jewish people, a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did you remain in the ghetto after that first liquidation? After the first liquidation, and you had working cards to stay in the ghetto, for how long did you stay after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till we went to Blizyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I marked it down, but I have it on the table, this. That was in 1944 or '43. We went to Blizyn. And that was in June-- I marked it down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported to Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, by, you know, those-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like what they are-- no, where they are transporting cows and pigs and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they open or were they closed cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Closed. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the scene at the railway station when you were going onto the trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe some people did react more than I did. I didn't know anything about-- we went in. If we had to go, we would have to go. That's all. That's what I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a smooth embarkation? Did the-- did the Nazis say, would you please get on the train? Or-- describe what took place.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, just with clubs and just get out and on and this. That wasn't smooth, not at all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was just the beating and beating, and that's all. We didn't know where we are going, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till we did arrive there in Blizyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the conditions in the railway car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very bad-- very, very bad, because there were a lot of people in one car. And the sanitary, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I personally had a need to go to the washroom and, yet, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any-- any water provided for the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Nothing was provided.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the train? To Blizyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was about five or six hours, something like this. It's not very far, but it took us a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded the train during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- also the Ukrainians and Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you with any of your family members at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother, Nojech.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had happened to the remainder of your family? Your parents, you had said, were deported during the liquidation. What about your older sister, Cluwa and Naftula, your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were deported, and they were sent to Treblinka before.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same action that your parents were sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Some in the same and some two days before. Like my sister Cluwa was transport-- was transported the 30th of November. And also--

speaker: interviewee
text: '43. Yeah, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, to Treblinka. And so the-- and we, after-- in 1944, we were transported to Blizyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying your sister Cluwa went to November 30. Would that have been 1943 or 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: When that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1943, '42, yeah, 1942, I've heard say. Everything gets mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop there and continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
